sentences.text: The first thing after the war I did is I volunteered to work in the stadt-house.
They needed somebody to record and see who left Antwerp.
And what I did is I-- they had at least 1,000 children who died in Antwerp, without anybody recording their names and everything.
But they had the shoes, just like in the concentration camps.
And I recorded names.
And I worked there for six weeks.
But then I couldn't take it.
I was very sad because I recognized a lot of names.
So I recorded and helped count the children-- record the names of those who died not in the concentration camps but in Antwerp.
After that, I said to my mother, I must have a job.
(LAUGHING) So she said, what are you going to do?
You don't know anything.
I went-- there were a few ads.
The American Army, I guess, wanted to help the Belgian people.
And one ad was they needed a secretary translating to work with the Germans' PLOs.
So I decided, well, I don't know how to type.
I've never typed 10 words, but I'll go for an interview.
I speak a little German, I speak Yiddish.
I'll mix it in, and I'll be fine.
I was interviewed by a Jewish captain who lives here in Philadelphia.
And as soon as he knew and talked with me and knew what I went through, he hired me.
Auschwitz-- uh, Auschpitz, from the Daley-- the Daleys on Bustleton Avenue and on Fifth.
He was one of them.
He interviewed me, and he hired me.
And I became his private typist-secretary.
But he had another one too.
And I also became his translator for the German PLO.
There were a lot of German PLOs who were working there.
Go ahead.
I said PLOs.
I'm sorry, thanks for correcting.
POWs.
And he used to say to me, why don't you punish them, if they used to do something wrong in the office or so?
I said, I can't.
Well, he used to punish them plenty.
But I worked with, for at least six months-- no, more-- as a secretary for the United States Army.
I had a very good job.
They were very nice to me.
I was the only Jew, of course.
The rest were Belgian people.
But they had to know German to be able to work with them.
And they were good typists.
I typed with one finger.
[LAUGHTER]
This was right after the war.
It was up till '46, January or February '46.
My husband, I met a month after the war, after the war was finished.
And I remember meeting him sitting in a little room, being in charge of 50-- maximum 50.
We never counted them.
I wish I would have-- boys and girls who remained alive in Antwerp.
We got together, we organized ourselves.
We called ourselves the Jewish Zionists.
And there was no division anymore of Labor Zionists, Reformed, Conservative, whatever-- the Jews.
And we had meetings together.
Now our meetings were very traumatic, because each one of us there was one or two who remained alive of a group.
The rest of them weren't there anymore.
They were all gone.
And we had a goal.
The goal was to tell the world, to show Belgium that we're not afraid, that we're coming out as Jews.
And the other goal was that-- in Belgium, they used to bring survivors from concentration camps to Antwerp.
There was a street that the Belgian government gave, and they could bring them there.
And we helped them.
We helped them.
And one day we were sitting and discussing, yes, go to Israel, not go to-- to Palestine there, illegally.
And from far away, a soldier opens the door, handsome fellow with a Red Cross thing, and walks in.
And then he couldn't speak too well Yiddish.
But in his broken Yiddish, he says, can he stay.
He came to see Jews.
And we said yes.
And he sat there and listened to our conversation.
When it was over-- I was the leader, I was in charge-- he came over.
And he said, could he come again?
I said, yes, but we are having a concert in a week, there and there, of all of us who got together.
My sister was giving the concert.
She was our voice and everything.
I said, if you can, come and listen to the concert.
So he came.
And he went over to Frieda.
And he said to her, your voice is beautiful, but you made a lot of mistakes in Hebrew.
[LAUGHTER]
So he wrote home for Hebrew books.
And his father-- they were very poor people here, by the way-- and his father sent a package of the first Hebrew songs, the first Hebrew books, and everything else.
And then he used to come to the meetings.
And one day he came over, and he said to me, could he come to my house?
So I had told my mother about this, of course.
And my mother said, yeah, you can let him come.
But don't ever walk with this soldier alone, your reputation is going to be gone.
I mean, with a soldier, an American soldier, you're going to walk?
So I listened to my mother.
So when I told her, I said, Mom, he would like to come for a Friday night meal.
So mother says, well, I'll make him the dry eggs.
I said, well, whatever we'll eat, he'll eat too.
So she says, OK.
First of all, she wanted to know, does he speak Yiddish.
I said, no, he speaks a little bit Yiddish.
But he speaks Hebrew very well.
I said, you can converse with him in Hebrew, you can have a conversation.
And then I said, you and I know English.
I knew 50 words, that's all, if I did know 50 words.
I don't know if I knew.
Anyway, then he started coming to our Friday night meals.
And he had to walk 6 miles to get to the Friday night's meal, from the depot to our home, to Hoboken.
And then he started bringing ration things that he got from-- and got from his soldiers.
He started bringing.
So Mama made the challah.
And Mama made the chicken.
And Mama made this.
And Frieda used to sing.
And Danny was in the Army Band also.
So I have three violins here from him.
So he used to bring the violin, and Frieda used to sing.
I don't have a voice, I can't sing.
I sing, because I love to sing, but-- and we used to have beautiful Friday night evenings.
Danny came for quite a few months in my house.
And I never went out with Danny once.
I was never alone with Danny.
Finally, one evening or so, he came.
And I said to my mother, I said, you know, I don't know if he ever saw Antwerp's underworld.
It's official there, the prostitutes.
You just go, and they sit in the windows.
And that's Europe.
It's in France like that.
It's in Belgium like that.
It's Holland.
So my mother said, OK, for a few hours, you can go to the Belgielei with him, Kaiserlei.
You know, can show him the opera and this and that.
So I went with Danny.
And I really went with him and showed him all of the underworld.
And we walked the streets.
And we had an awful lot in common.
I mean, whatever subject he brought up, I had too.
I had a little less knowledge because I didn't have knowledge.
I mean, I was in hiding.
But I knew.
What he saw from us-- he kept on telling me about America.
I shouldn't think America is a rich country.
It's rich for certain people.
He told me about his parents, how poor they are and how the drugstore barely they can live on it.
And he lives in a neighborhood, he said, not like-- I showed him my neighborhood.
That's not the neighborhood he lives in.
And he used to tell me all the reality of America.
And he used to speak a lot about this father and mother.
Now he was an only child, of course.
Yeah, and then I took him to a cafe, a very beautiful cafe right in the heart of Antwerp, where the violinist plays beautiful music and so on.
And I told the violinist what I want him to play for me.
And I'll never forget that song, 'Solveig's Song,' Grieg's 'Solveig's Song.' I don't know if you know it.
It says, the winter [GERMAN].
The seasons can go and come, but your love will always stay with me.
It's a very beautiful Grieg song.
Edvard Grieg wrote it in German.
And I knew it from when I was so, my mother used to sing it to me.
So first of all, Danny picked that up.
I mean, Danny had very good-- he didn't need music.
I mean, he used to play the Sibelius violin concerto like nothing, without music.
And I came home, and Danny went back to the depot.
And I came into the house.
And my mother asked me, how was it.
It was late at night already, and so on.
I said, Mom, I'm going to marry this man.
So she looked at me.
She said, you schnook, little kid.
And she says to me, did he ask you?
I said, no, he didn't ask me.
I'm just telling you that I will marry him.
(LAUGHING) And the truth came out.
Danny was settled in Antwerp.
And I really don't know.
He always said to me, I never asked you, you never asked me.
But all at once, we planned the wedding.
But he kept on saying, remember, you're going to a very poor place.
My parents have nothing, I have nothing.
I'm going back to the University of Pennsylvania.
You'll have to work like a horse.
You're getting nothing.
So I used to say, I'm getting you.
And that's-- anyway, when Antwerp Jews got to know that there's going to be a Jewish wedding, everybody started participating in that wedding.
Every family that was alive or person that was alive made a cake.
One was decorated with a hammer and sickle.
And one was decorated with a Jewish star.
And one was-- whatever you want were the cakes.
The American Army gave us their band.
I had an honor guard of the American Army.
And we got married.
His father and his mother, I'm sure, were very, very disappointed.
Here is this 19-year-old boy who is a very bright fellow, who is marrying they don't even know who, a survivor.
And what's he going to come home to, bring her home to-- I mean.
There's no-- he's going to get $90 on the GI Bill of Rights.
We were married.
Our honeymoon, by the way, was in Ostend, where we were running for.
The honeymoon was, Danny had in his knapsack what his Army officer and the friends gave him, of cigarettes and booze and all of this.
And he sold it all, and that's how we got a room and food.
We had no money.
And when we came back, Danny had applied, in a joke-- he didn't know that it would ever happen to him.
The United States Army was sending 2,000 geniuses to Biarritz, to study for two weeks, college courses, to remember that you are still in college.
And Danny was picked.
And here I'm married two weeks or three weeks.
And Danny says he's not going.
And I say, yes, you're going.
This is an opportunity of a lifetime.
And this is the Army.
And he went.
I have a lot of letters from him.
That's going to be in the book.
And he came back.
And we got a telegram from his parents that his father is very, very sick, and that the Army wants to bring him back home.
Because he was an only child, so he can come back home.
And here I am.
I just came out from hiding.
I told you, I had nothing in my life.
All at once, I found somebody that I really have-- Danny.
And I feel I'm losing him.
Not I'm losing him because he doesn't want me anymore, but wherever I go, something is happening.
And he shows me this letter, this telegram.
I think I have it on the table.
And I say, you have to go home.
Your father needs you.
And, when are you going to come?
Whenever the Army will bring me, I'll come.
You know, I was a war bride.
I was away from Danny for six months, separated after only being married for a month or so.
We wrote to each other every day.
His father wrote to me love letters, what his son is telling him about me.
His mother, I never got a letter from.
Danny, when he came back-- his father was very, very sick-- started working for the Zionist movement-- went back to the University of Pennsylvania, of course, after he was discharged.
And got there his master's, his bachelor's, his PhD, whatever.
And he wrote to me everything.
I mean, every day.
But the meantime, I was sitting on Stadien Street, on the steps, waiting every day for a letter from Danny.
If the letter, god forbid, didn't come that day and the next day came too, I was back in the Holocaust and maybe worse.
My mother and all of us had to worry for every day a piece of bread still, because we didn't get yet anything.
Later on we got a little help.
They gave everybody a little help, the Jewish gemeinde.
And my wedding, if I would describe it, would take over a half hour, maybe more.
But I felt, and so did Danny feel, through my letters, through his letters that I read, that we're going to have a very rich life together.
When I came here, I went to high school, college, and I started working.
So did he.
And we built a very beautiful life.
We didn't want too much.
We just wanted each other.
And there too, I think because I'm a survivor, I was able to survive so many things.
He died, he was 57 years old.
When I finally felt that I have something, and that I am-- I can live, I can do, then my husband died.
Yes?
I came March 1946-- I'm sorry. 1945, I was married.
I came to America, March 1946.
Sorry.
We have a son.
His name is Yonathan.
He's an adopted child.
For years-- I mean, when I came here, every doctor that I went to, and Danny-- there was nothing wrong with us.
They felt that the Holocaust did this to me.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] We talk about surviving.
I think that we survivors-- I really think, and maybe it's not true, but I think it is.

I don't think that an average or even a very bright American girl who wouldn't have gone through the Holocaust, what I went through, would have been able to take so much and not break down, or not something happen.
I am a lucky person.
After Danny died, and before he died-- he died from a heart attack.
He was president of Gratz College.
I know that he was loved by everybody, by his students, by his faculty.
I have diplomas and things from them, and letters and everything.
I could have done two things in life after he died.
One, start crying and give up and say, what do I need it all for?
It's gone.
Enough is enough.
I took the other way, the other road-- Dorothy, with the roads.
I took the road that-- Danny wouldn't have wanted me to do that.
And I wrote books.
I teach.
I lecture.
I miss a lot of things.
Danny and I used to go-- Danny knew that I loved the opera.
So instead of going wherever, once a month, Saturday afternoon, we went to New York to the opera, had dinner, and saw the opera, and came home.
We had tickets to the concerts here in Philadelphia twice a week, Saturday night concert.
I had to finish with that too.
There are a lot of things that I miss.
But they're things that I have had a lot also.
So I miss it.
But I know I had it.
Danny and I used to travel every summer for a week to one country.
So we really saw Europe.
But only every summer, after I took students to Israel, he met me in a country, like in Italy, in France, whatever.
Because he couldn't go to Israel for six and a half weeks with the Gratz College students.
I went, I went for 13 years, every summer, and traveled and really enjoyed it and had a wonderful time.
America, I saw a lot.
But California, when I used to say, I want to see California-- we have time.
Well, we didn't have time.
I hope I'll do it without him one day.
But I think too, I learned lessons of life through the Holocaust that made me, I think, and some of us survivors.
I don't think I'm an exception to the rule.
Maybe I am in certain ways.
That you can't give up.
Because if you give up, you become that poor Clara, or that poor Mrs.
Isaacman.
What is she doing?
I'm also lucky that-- I can count them on one hand.
But I have a friend.
I have two friends.
And the Gratz College Board, when Danny died, was very supportive.
Gratz then didn't have life insurance, didn't have pension fund, didn't have anything.
So I remained a poor widow but a rich widow, very rich.
I wouldn't change it for millions.
But it was life that's teaching me that.
And I think that our messages and our videos and all of this, if we don't teach people about those things, then we are only telling a story.
I don't want mine to become a story.
I want it to become a lesson.
I want it to become something that people will be able to learn from and say, hey, she could do it.
I can do it too.
And that was part of my meeting Danny, living with Danny, and having wonderful days and years with Danny.
Oh, yeah, that was very, very-- Danny, in 1962, organized-- Danny used to be in charge, we used to be in charge here of a camp.
Danny bought a Zionist camp in Galil, in Bucks County.
It belongs to the Zionist movement.
It doesn't belong to anybody.
He went and begged a few rich people.
They paid for it.
And it's 60 acres in Doylestown, near Ottsville.
And every summer, there are 200 kids there who are learning about Zionism, Israel, co-operative living, Judaism.
Rabbi Albert Storer goes there.
Then Danny became president of Gratz.
Although, Danny was first a teacher at Gratz.
Then he was a principal of Gratz.
Then he was administrator.
And then he became president of Gratz.
And he decided that it's time for Jewish students to go to Israel and see what Israel is.
And he went to Israel, and he made a program of a six and a half week program-- study and travel.
And every summer, 80, 90 students from Gratz and from the synagogues all over the city of Philadelphia-- Cheltenham, Abington, all over-- went with Gratz College to Israel.
But they had to study too.
It wasn't just travel.
And there was a certain program.
And I went for 14 years, every summer, with a professor, because there had to be professorship too-- not enough teacher, because they got credit.
Even Penn gives them credit.
And we studied and traveled.
I went up Masada 100 times.
[LAUGHING] And that was the program.
